impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting judgment as a matter of law that Teva's headache patents claiming methods of treating headache using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity of Teva's headache patents, reinstating the jury's findings of willful infringement by Eli Lilly. The court found error in the district court's written-description and enablement analysis for claims covering use of humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies to treat headache. This decision has major implications for pharmaceutical patent scope covering biologic drug classes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the pharmaceutical and biologics space benefit from a more permissive standard for written description and enablement of genus claims, particularly where the specification discloses methods of making the claimed compounds and the prior art supports the genus.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors like Eli Lilly face renewed infringement liability and potential damages for products in the lucrative migraine treatment market, and generic/biosimilar entrants face continued patent barriers.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the 'nonstaple goods' defense applies when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, holding that Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of its unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court rejected Ingevity's 'nonstaple goods' defense because the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patented technology. This decision reinforces limits on leveraging patent rights to foreclose competition in adjacent product markets.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors benefit from strong precedent preventing patent holders from using licensing agreements to lock up sales of unpatented products where those products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when structuring exclusive supply arrangements tied to patent licenses, even for products closely related to the patented technology.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are directed to an abstract idea under Alice step one, and whether the district court erred in awarding damages based on foreign sales of infringing software.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patent claims are abstract under Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. The decision narrows the enforceability of software patents covering virus/malware detection methods and limits extraterritorial patent damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from a strong ruling limiting patent eligibility for software-based security detection methods and restricting damages for foreign sales, reducing exposure in patent litigation.
impacts.negativeImplications: University patent holders and other entities with software method patents face heightened invalidity risk under § 101 and reduced damages potential when accused products are sold globally.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming media patents were ineligible under § 101, reversing the jury's infringement verdict in favor of GoTV and entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to a device's capabilities. This ruling significantly impacts patent enforcement in the streaming media and content delivery space.
impacts.positiveImplications: Major technology and streaming companies like Netflix gain protection from patent assertions based on broad claims covering content adaptation for different devices, a common feature of modern streaming services.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the media streaming and content delivery sectors face increased difficulty enforcing patents directed to adaptive content delivery, even where claims recite specific server-side operations.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court correctly granted summary judgment of noninfringement of multicore processor patent claims on extraterritoriality grounds, and whether the district court erred in striking the patent holder's damages expert theories.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed in part the district court's summary judgment of noninfringement of VLSI's multicore processor patent against Intel, finding that the extraterritoriality analysis was improperly applied. The court affirmed on other grounds and remanded for further proceedings. This case addresses the important question of when U.S. patent infringement occurs for processor designs that may be manufactured or configured abroad.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a ruling that limits the use of extraterritoriality defenses to avoid infringement liability for products sold and used in the United States, even when some steps occur abroad.
impacts.negativeImplications: Semiconductor and technology companies face renewed infringement exposure where accused products are designed or partially configured overseas but ultimately sold and used domestically.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the plaintiff presented legally sufficient evidence that its asserted trade secrets were protectable under California law, and whether generally known information can constitute an inventive contribution for patent inventorship purposes.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the jury's trade secret misappropriation verdict, holding that plaintiffs failed to prove their asserted trade secrets were protectable because the information was generally known in the field. The court also reversed the patent invalidity finding because the alleged inventor's contribution of generally known ideas cannot constitute an inventive contribution. This decision clarifies the evidentiary bar for trade secret protection and its intersection with patent inventorship.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in trade secret cases benefit from a rigorous standard requiring plaintiffs to prove that asserted trade secrets are not generally known, providing a stronger basis for JMOL motions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies asserting trade secret claims face heightened scrutiny of whether their purported secrets are truly novel and not generally known in the industry, particularly where overlapping patent claims exist.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both Tenaris's inequitable conduct in obtaining its coiled tubing patents and Global Tubing's Walker Process antitrust fraud claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves allegations that Tenaris withheld prior art (CYMAX Documents) during patent prosecution. This decision sends both the inequitable conduct defense and the antitrust Walker Process claim to trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to find genuine factual disputes on inequitable conduct and Walker Process claims, preserving these important defenses and counterclaims for trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased litigation risk where prior art acquired through asset purchases was not disclosed during prosecution, potentially exposing them to both unenforceability and antitrust liability.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated § 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watches that infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement technology, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing models. The court found no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings. This is a commercially significant decision affecting Apple's flagship product line.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with validated ITC exclusion orders gain strong enforcement tools against major importers, with the Federal Circuit providing deference to ITC findings on domestic industry and infringement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies integrating health-monitoring features into consumer products face significant import bans and must navigate patent clearance for increasingly sophisticated sensor technologies.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative testimony may be considered at summary judgment regardless of the witness's personal knowledge, and whether the on-sale bar under 35 U.S.C. § 102(b) requires that the prior art sale expressly disclose the device's functionality.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity under the pre-AIA § 102(b) on-sale bar, holding that 30(b)(6) corporate representative testimony is admissible at summary judgment even without personal knowledge, and that the on-sale bar does not require express disclosure of a device's functionality in the sale itself. The court also addressed the treatment of source code as non-hearsay evidence.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers gain a powerful tool for proving prior sales, as 30(b)(6) testimony about historical sales is admissible regardless of the witness's personal involvement, and prior art sales need not disclose functionality.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face broader invalidation risk under the on-sale bar, as defendants can prove prior sales through corporate designees who lack firsthand knowledge, lowering the evidentiary burden for invalidity.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action when it previously missed the deadline for seeking a stay of the original action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that a party cannot circumvent the statutory deadline for seeking a mandatory stay of a parallel district court action by voluntarily dismissing and refiling the same declaratory judgment action. This prevents gaming of § 1659(a)'s timing requirements to force a mandatory stay of district court proceedings pending an ITC investigation. The decision is significant for pharmaceutical and technology companies managing parallel ITC and district court patent disputes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from protection against strategic refiling of declaratory judgment actions designed to exploit mandatory stay provisions and delay district court proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day stay deadline cannot cure the deficiency by refiling, reducing their ability to coordinate parallel proceedings and forcing them to litigate on multiple fronts simultaneously.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent holder's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft, finding that the district court applied an overly restrictive standard in evaluating the expert's methodology. The court held that the damages expert's approach to quantifying benefits of alleged infringement was sufficiently reliable. This decision lowers the bar for patent plaintiffs' damages evidence to survive Daubert challenges.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more permissive standard for damages expert testimony, making it harder for defendants to eliminate patent cases entirely through Daubert motions targeting damages evidence.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies face reduced ability to defeat patent infringement claims at summary judgment by challenging damages expert methodologies, potentially increasing litigation costs and settlement pressure.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million nationwide class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER's operation.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million class settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary exceeded its statutory authority by charging PACER fees to fund non-PACER expenses. The court upheld the settlement, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representatives, rejecting objections regarding jurisdiction, settlement fairness, and the propriety of incentive awards. This decision resolves a significant dispute over government fee practices affecting hundreds of thousands of court record users.
impacts.positiveImplications: The settlement provides substantial reimbursement to PACER users and establishes that class action mechanisms can effectively challenge government overcharging, with the Federal Circuit providing a clear framework for settlement approval.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions on IPR institution discretion are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, that the instructions do not bind the Director, and that non-institution decisions are insulated from judicial review. This preserves the Director's broad discretion to decline IPR institution.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from continued PTO flexibility to deny IPR petitions under discretionary frameworks without being constrained by formal rulemaking processes.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners face continued uncertainty from discretionary denials of IPR institution, with no ability to challenge the policy framework through administrative law procedural requirements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records, and whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is consistent with the governing statute.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records in trade proceedings, and that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute. The court affirmed the CIT's order directing the ITC to comply with the statute's confidentiality requirements. This decision increases transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries and the public gain greater access to information in trade remedy proceedings, enhancing transparency and the ability to scrutinize ITC determinations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit questionnaire responses in ITC investigations face potential public disclosure of competitively sensitive business information that was previously automatically treated as confidential.
